title: Believe the hands
summary: A pediatric nurse retiring after 37 years gives her replacement two days of training. She tells him the things that are not in the manual.

body.speaker: Aida
body.text: Just Aida. I retire on Friday. We have today and tomorrow.

body.speaker: Aida
body.text: Thirty-seven years on this ward. I will tell you the things they do not write in the manual.

body.speaker: Aida
body.text: One. The children. They are not small adults. They lie about pain in the other direction — they say it hurts less than it does, not more. Because they have learned that the more pain they admit, the more procedures we do. Watch the eyes. Watch the hands. Believe the hands.

body.speaker: Aida
body.text: Two. The parents. The angry ones are the easy ones. They are afraid. Speak slowly. Tell the truth. The dangerous ones are the quiet ones. The quiet ones have stopped speaking because they have decided they cannot help. They need to be invited back into the room.

body.speaker: Aida
body.text: Ask them a question only they can answer. 'What does she usually eat at home?' 'What time does she normally fall asleep?' Then write the answer down where they can see you writing it.

body.speaker: Aida
body.text: Three. Yourself. You will lose a patient in the first eighteen months. Not because of anything you do. Because this is a children's ward and some children die. When it happens, you will want to leave the profession. Do not decide that day. Decide six months later.

body.speaker: Aida
body.text: Each one is a name I still say to myself in the car on the way home, on a particular date in the year. The driving home was the worst part. Get a driver, or take the bus.

body.speaker: Aida
body.text: Good. Now. Wash your hands. We start at six.

annotations.meaningVi: thủ thuật y tế (xét nghiệm, can thiệp)
sổ tay / cẩm nang hướng dẫn

grammarNotes.title: Imperatives + 'will' for prediction in instruction
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng mệnh lệnh và 'will + V' xen kẽ khi dạy việc — mệnh lệnh cho hành động, 'will' cho dự đoán trải nghiệm. Trong bài: 'Watch the eyes. Watch the hands. Believe the hands.' (mệnh lệnh). 'You will lose a patient in the first eighteen months.' (dự đoán). 'When it happens, you will want to leave the profession.' (dự đoán cảm xúc). 'Do not decide that day. Decide six months later.' (mệnh lệnh). Kỹ thuật này vừa hướng dẫn hành vi, vừa chuẩn bị tinh thần.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 mixes imperatives with 'will + verb' when teaching — imperatives for actions, 'will' for predicting experience. The dialogue: 'Watch the eyes. Watch the hands. Believe the hands.' (commands). 'You will lose a patient in the first eighteen months.' (prediction). 'When it happens, you will want to leave the profession.' (predicted feeling). 'Do not decide that day. Decide six months later.' (instruction). Together they guide behaviour and prepare emotion.

translationVi:
Aida: Tomas. Ngồi đi. Tôi là Aida.
Tomas: Vâng, y tá Aida.
Aida: Chỉ Aida thôi. Tôi nghỉ hưu thứ Sáu. Mình có hôm nay và ngày mai.
Tomas: Vâng, Aida.
Aida: Ba mươi bảy năm ở khoa này. Tôi sẽ nói với cậu những điều không có trong cẩm nang.
Tomas: Tôi sẵn sàng.
Aida: Một. Trẻ con. Chúng không phải người lớn thu nhỏ. Chúng nói dối về cơn đau theo chiều ngược — chúng bảo đau ít hơn thực tế, không phải nhiều hơn. Vì chúng đã học rằng càng thú nhận đau nhiều, mình càng làm nhiều thủ thuật. Nhìn vào mắt. Nhìn vào tay. Tin tay.
Tomas: Tin tay.
Aida: Hai. Cha mẹ. Người tức là người dễ. Họ sợ. Nói chậm. Nói thật. Người nguy hiểm là người im. Người im đã thôi nói vì họ kết luận rằng họ không giúp được. Họ cần được mời lại vào trong phòng.
Tomas: Tôi mời thế nào?
Aida: Hỏi họ một câu chỉ họ trả lời được. 'Ở nhà cháu thường ăn gì?' 'Cháu thường ngủ lúc mấy giờ?' Rồi ghi câu trả lời ở chỗ họ thấy mình ghi.
Tomas: ... Tôi hiểu.
Aida: Ba. Chính cậu. Cậu sẽ mất một bệnh nhân trong mười tám tháng đầu. Không phải vì điều cậu làm. Vì đây là khoa nhi và một số trẻ qua đời. Khi điều đó xảy ra, cậu sẽ muốn rời nghề. Đừng quyết hôm đó. Quyết sáu tháng sau.
Tomas: Cô đã mất bao nhiêu?
Aida: Hai mươi tám.
Tomas: Hai mươi tám.
Aida: Mỗi tên là một cái tên tôi vẫn nói thầm trong xe trên đường về nhà, vào một ngày nhất định trong năm. Lái xe về là phần tệ nhất. Tìm người chở, hoặc đi xe buýt.
Tomas: Tôi sẽ đi xe buýt.
Aida: Tốt. Giờ. Rửa tay. Mình bắt đầu lúc sáu.

questions.prompt: How long has Aida worked on the ward?
What does Aida say about how children lie about pain?
options: They say it hurts less than it does — because they have learned that more pain means more procedures.

questions.prompt: What part of the child should Tomas watch and believe?
options: The eyes; the hands — 'believe the hands.'
questions.explanation: 'Watch the eyes. Watch the hands. Believe the hands.'

questions.prompt: Which parents does Aida call 'easy', and which 'dangerous'?
options: Easy: the angry ones (they are afraid); dangerous: the quiet ones (they have decided they cannot help).
Easy: the silent ones; dangerous: the loud ones.

questions.prompt: How does Aida tell Tomas to 'invite' quiet parents back?
options: Ask a question only they can answer; then write the answer down where they can see you writing it.

questions.prompt: What does Aida predict about Tomas's first eighteen months?
options: He will lose a patient — not from anything he does. He will want to leave the profession. He should not decide that day.
He will never lose a patient.
He will move to another country.

questions.prompt: When should Tomas decide whether to stay?
options: Six months later, not that day

questions.prompt: How many patients has Aida lost?
What does Aida say about driving home?
options: It was the worst part — Tomas should get a driver or take the bus.

questions.prompt: Why was driving home the worst part?
options: Each lost child's name comes back on a particular date — she still says them to herself in the car.
questions.explanation: Aida's reply about names in the car.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That a nurse can avoid losing any patient with enough skill.
That quiet parents can be 'invited back' with the right question.
That decisions about leaving the profession should not be made on the day of a loss.
questions.explanation: 'You will lose a patient… not because of anything you do.'
questions.hint: Look for the option Aida explicitly refuses.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: The most important parts of long professional practice are emotional protocols that no manual contains — and they can be handed over deliberately to the next person.
questions.explanation: Aida's three lessons are not procedural but emotional/perceptual.
questions.hint: Notice that none of the three lessons is in any manual.

cloze.template:
Aida: Tomas. Sit. I am Aida.
Tomas: Yes, Nurse Aida.
Aida: Just Aida. I retire on Friday. We have today and tomorrow.
Tomas: Yes, Aida.
Aida: Thirty-{{b1}} years on this ward. I will tell you the things they do not write in the {{b2}}.
Tomas: I am ready.
Aida: One. The children. They are not small adults. They lie about pain in the other direction — they say it hurts {{b3}} than it does, not more. Because they have learned that the more pain they admit, the more procedures we do. Watch the eyes. Watch the hands. {{b4}} the hands.
Tomas: Believe the hands.
Aida: Two. The parents. The angry ones are the easy ones. They are {{b5}}. Speak slowly. Tell the truth. The dangerous ones are the {{b6}} ones. The quiet ones have stopped speaking because they have decided they cannot help. They need to be invited back into the room.
Tomas: How do I invite them?
Aida: Ask them a question only they can answer. 'What does she usually eat at home?' 'What time does she normally fall asleep?' Then write the answer down where they can see you writing it.
Tomas: … I see.
Aida: Three. Yourself. You will lose a patient in the first eighteen {{b7}}. Not because of anything you do. Because this is a children's ward and some children die. When it happens, you will want to leave the profession. Do not decide that day. Decide {{b8}} months later.
Tomas: How many have you lost?
Aida: Twenty-{{b9}}.
Tomas: Twenty-eight.
Aida: Each one is a name I still say to myself in the car on the way home, on a particular date in the year. The driving home was the worst part. Get a driver, or take the {{b10}}.
Tomas: I will take the bus.
Aida: Good. Now. Wash your hands. We start at {{b11}}.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Aida tells Tomas to delay any decision about leaving the profession by six months. Is there a decision in your own life that you have made (or are about to make) on a hard day, when six months later would be a different person making it? What did you decide — or could you still wait?
